data.title: QQH: Look Beyond The Marketing, This ETF Has Big Flaws
data.description:
Investors are subjected to so much hype and bravado, they can be excused for missing some of the important "due diligence" points that are not always apparent. I am no different, and I get fooled too. But hopefully after 30 years of ETF, stock and macro market analysis, I've reached the point where my mantra of "avoid big loss" remains intact.

Beware the bull market in "shiny objects"

This is a market environment characterized by amazing tales of investing styles that seem too good to be true. Most are. That's not a cynical view, because no one can blame ETF companies for trying. Among the "alternative" investment approaches I find most overhyped compared to their actual ability to avoid major losses are so-called "buffered" ETFs. These cushion the initial decline in bear markets, but leave the door open for significant asset bleeding. They are not the subject of this article, and I'll take my shot at some of them in the coming weeks.

The topic here is Howard Capital's HCM Defender 100 Index ETF (NYSEARCA:QQH), which is similar to a mutual fund that preceded it by several years, dating back to 2014. That allows us to judge it, not in a sustained bear market (since we haven't had one during that time) but over some shorter bursts where it went lower, including those in late 2018, early 2020 and the first 9 months of 2022.

The idea of QQH is straightforward: separate time into 2 separate conditions. Those when the Nasdaq 100 is going up and those when it is going down. The flatter periods are, as I see it, grouped with the up periods. The fund's 2 main weapons are Nasdaq-oriented ETFs and stocks, and T-bills or similar cash equivalents. This is a true "market timing" strategy.

What is "market timing" anyway?

Marketing timing is dirty word in some investing circles. Ironically, it is dismissed by some of the very people who practice it. They just don't consider what they do to be market timing. I'm talking about the "quarterly rebalancing" crowd, as in owning an allegedly diversified mix of stock and bond asset styles, and at the same time each calendar quarter, selling down the winners and buying more of the losers to reset each asset class to its "model weight." This is part science, part marketing scheme and part attempt to keep fee-based assets from fleeing, under the guise of "Nobel Prize-winning" investing theory, which is now nearly 50 years old and doesn't work at all like it used to. But I digress.

Marketing timing is part of investing. That is, whatever investment one is making, the time at which the investment process is executed is determined and plotted. Even if it is semi-automated.

With QQH, I have zero issue with the attempt to shift from stocks to cash or vice versa. All I care about is if it works. To some, "works" means that it gets its fair share of the up cycles, and really clamps down on rough markets. Somewhere along the way, losing 20% of one's peak portfolio value became acceptable. Not to me, and I assume not to my subscribers.

Defend? Against what?

QQH's timing mechanism, like the mutual fund version that preceded it, has a mixed record so far. I would expect to see regular cases of limiting losses that separate the ETF from QQQ. Nope. Sometimes it defends as the name says, sometimes it doesn't.

Over time, QQH has done quite well, nearly equaling QQQ the past 5 years. However, this is not a period in which the hedging mechanism was truly stress tested in terms of a long-lived decline. And when sudden market breaks did occur, it gave up plenty of territory.

Drilling down, and using the mutual fund version to go back 10 years instead of 5, QQH has lost 20% or close to that on several occasions...in just 3 months' time! As a career risk manager, who was OK with not making 30-60% in a single year in exchange for "avoiding big loss" as I defined it, this is outside the lines for me.

And below, there is obviously a mechanism at work to stem some of the worst losses. But that purple line exceeds 20% too often, and in 2022 this strategy lost about 40%, which was notably more than QQQ.

I'm all for taking emotion out of the equation, as this graphic from the firm's website states. But a 40% loss from peak to trough indicates there is some whipsaw impact on the "secret sauce" here, known as the HCM-Buy-Line.

Despite this, and maybe due to the fact that Howard Capital has an investment advisory firm, whose client base may account for a chunk of that $586mm in assets, QQH is thriving from a business standpoint. A 1% expense ratio on that AUM, do the math, that's good living!

The holdings' allocation by sector below indicates that QQQ-like focus, and the table below it shows that a combination of ETFs and much of the 100 stocks within QQQ, owned directly here, are the "risk on" asset basket for QQH.

Actually, there is some leverage at play here, since the top holding, nearly 20% of the fund, is in a 3x QQQ ETF.

Finally, zeroing in on QQH's worst period, as we should do to stress test any ETF that aims to manage risk actively, this says it all. From the start of 2022 to 27 months later (3/31/24), QQQ dove and recovered. It netted a 13% return, or about 6% annualized. Not bad considering we had the dawn of a new bond bear market that also hit stocks hard, temporarily.

But the whole idea of managing risk and not just "buying and holding" and hoping the market and time will bail us out is that each new up cycle can start with close to what we had when things turned south. But when you get 40% in the hole as QQH did here, it costs time as much as money. Because even when the money is nearly recovered, the time is lost forever.

QQH: I say no way, for my portfolio

Every investor chooses their own path. For me, since my whole approach is centered around being competitive in any market conditions, QQH is not a value-add for me. I used inverse ETFs (single, double and triple at times), put options and tactical positioning, rather than a secret formula. It takes some work, but that's what the investing group I run is about.

Others might find value here. I just don't see it. And while QQH will do just fine when the Nasdaq 100 appreciates, it is not worth the risk versus alternative approaches. This ETF is far from the only one that doesn't deliver on the idea of true risk management. I've been around that style for decades and the more one tries to "get away with" capturing most of the upside in the good times, the less margin for error they have in unfriendly markets.

So based on my lack of confidence in QQH to play a role in my portfolio's "department of defense," I rate it a sell.

data.title: Trading Nebius Group's Hypergrowth And Hyper-Volatility
data.description:
We had issued a buy rating on Nebius Group N.V. (NASDAQ:NBIS) in the high teens at our investing service. We have yet to cover it publicly, but in this column, we reiterate a buy and specifically suggest scooping shares on big dips. This is a very volatile stock and one that swing traders can do very well in. However, we also view it as a long-term winner. Now, make no mistake, if you apply traditional valuation analysis to the stock, like so many other hypergrowth names, it simply looks silly and expensive. But the company and the stock are set to grow into this growth. For the uninitiated, this is at its core, a technology holding company if you will, with a focus on AI infrastructure. It is building out and providing that infrastructure for a number of AI applications and developers, including large-scale GPU and cloud platforms. This is the core business, but also has three subsegment partner businesses in Toloka, TripleTen, and Avride. These work on generative AI development, tech career skill building, and autonomous vehicles, respectively.

The stock has surged since late 2024 after undergoing significant restructuring. The stock today is falling on just reported Q4 earnings, and in this column, we discuss the results.

Let us first hit the numbers. First, the company is in hypergrowth mode, but the revenues came up short against consensus estimates. When a stock is priced for absolute perfection, coming up short can lead to selling, which we are seeing. The company reported revenue of $37.9 million, which was significantly short of estimates by $19 million. However, it increased 466% year over year. This was a big result of the work being done in its core AI infrastructure business. That line of business grew 602% year-over-year alone. Now, like so many other companies in hyper-revenue growth mode, it loses money. Adjusted EBITDA loss was $75.5 million, while the net loss for Q4 was $136.6 million. We are seeing a similar trend in the annual results. The company saw 2024 revenue of $117.5 million which increased 462% year-over-year, while adjusted EBITDA loss was $266.4 million and net loss for the year was $396.9 million.

That said, it is cash-rich here right now. Cash and equivalents were $2.45 billion at the end of the year. That provides a long runway to continue expansion efforts. For the year, the company spent $808 million on capex. We want to point out, too, that ARR is continuing to expand thanks to past deals and current deals in the pipeline. We learned from management that for the month of March 2025, it will push $220 million.

However, it was weak for December 2024 at just $90 million, and short of expectations. But it is not about where the company has been, it is about where it is going. We see ARR ramping up. That is strong momentum as data center capacity ramps up and Nvidia's (NVDA) Blackwell comes full to market. By the end of the year, ARR could reach up to $1 billion. This is massive growth.

So, we see the core AI business brings in more than half of revenue. What about the other partner segments? These segments are still in growth mode. Toloka's 2024 revenue was up 140%. Keep in mind that Toloka shifted its business model in 2025 to focus more on generative AI-related training, which helped diversify its client base. TripleTen's 2024 revenue doubled year-over-year. This is a win. There was a 149% increase in enrolled students to over 14,000. And in Q4, over 4,000 students were added.

Then, of course, Avride, the autonomous vehicle partner, recently announced a pivotal partnership with Grubhub. This will enable autonomous delivery of food on many college campuses in the U.S. As of January of this year, over 100 autonomous robots were in operation delivering food, and this is expected to grow. Keep in mind, that there are also partnerships with Uber Eats (UBER) as well. And there is a lot of international opportunity here, with Avride's autonomous robots receiving necessary certification in Japan. It is also tested in South Korea. These are very populous areas for future growth.

As we look ahead to 2025, we expect ongoing rampant growth in revenues and more and more customers. The company has made solid progress toward expanding its AI infrastructure footprint. We can expect GPU cluster deployments, and adoption of its cloud platforms, in both the U.S. and Europe. The number of contracts continues to grow, and this is leading to an expanding ARR. While specific numbers are difficult to project, we think revenue can increase another 300-400% this year as a whole. Naturally, it is a long year ahead and there are many moving parts, but the company is clearly in growth mode, hypergrowth at that.

Over time, we would expect Nebius Group N.V. to lose less money and move toward breakeven. While execution risk is paramount, we think significant money can be made by both swing traders buying on dips, and investors looking for long-term returns. Wait for the pullbacks, like we are seeing today, and pick your spots.

data.title: Wix.com's Q4 Earnings: The Market Might Have Overreacted
data.description:
Today's analysis looks at Wix.com Ltd.'s (NASDAQ:WIX) stock, an asset that has surged by over 130% since our latest coverage in February 2023. While Wix's has posted stellar returns, it remains relatively thinly talked about among analysts and the media alike, and therefore, we decided to weigh in with a revision of the SaaS platform's prospects, especially in light of its Q4 earnings release on Wednesday, February 19th.

Our previous analysis assigned a 'buy rating' on the basis of Wix would benefit from SME growth and efficiency gains. While we maintain our base case, we've spotted additional talking points; here are our latest thoughts.

Accrual-Based Earnings Results

Wix reported mixed fourth-quarter results. Its revenue rose by 14% year-over-year to $460.5 million; however, expectations were unmatched as the figure settled $930 000 below expectations. While Wix surpassed its earnings-per-share estimates by 32 cents, the website builder's stock slid by low-to-mid single digits when the market opened on Wednesday morning, suggesting that investors are pessimistic about the company's quarterly results and outlook.

Here's a summary of Wix's 2025 full-year outlook.

Fundamental Review

Wix recorded $465 million in bookings during its fourth-quarter, a $70 million increase from a year earlier. The company's momentum derived from both its business and creative subscription segments, where the prior refers to commerce-related tools and the latter to a suite of website hosting and design features.

We expect Wix's growth to sustain for the foreseeable future with our basis being robust industry growth and Wix's noteworthy market position. More specifically, Wix owns about 9.5% of the website builder market, providing it with a solid foundation to leverage the website hosting and e-commerce markets, which are forecasted to compound by 20.2% and 26.5% per year until 2030.

Product pricing might become more competitive in years to come. In fact, we've already seen this occur. For example, Hostinger (among other outlets), which is a close proxy to Wix, provides a product for under $10 per month, a significant reduction from Wix's entry-level product, which starts at around $17 per month. However, despite the brewing price competition, Wix has established a solid brand, illustrated by its net revenue retention rate of around 106%. Therefore, abrupt customer product switching is unlikely, and an even more unlikely scenario would be one where other website builders have discovered cost-reduction strategies that Wix can't implement by itself, unless Wix's competitors decide to use cannibalization tactics, of course.

Furthermore, Wix's gross profit margins remained above 30% in Q4 and its net income settled at a rounded number of $48.02 million, a significant increase from the rounded $2.95 million achieved a year earlier. A closer look at the company's indirect cost base shows that most costs remained flat year-over-year, which is encouraging to see as it conveys potential for continued margin expansion. Sure, Wix's direct costs will probably fluctuate concurrent to sales; however, capacity exists for economies of scale if the company's market share increases.

Aside: Notice how Wix's Q4 and 2024 full-year results correlated, showing a broader growth pattern instead of an isolated quarterly shift.

A full-year vantage point was assumed to assess the company's balance sheet and cash flow. Wix is free cash flow positive, providing it with plenty of scope for capital reinvestment, all else equal. However, we are a tad worried about Wix's balance sheet; I calculated the company's current ratio to be 0.85x, which suggests a drag on liquidity might occur if current assets don't catch up to current liabilities. In addition, Wix has about $572.88 million in net convertible notes outstanding, forming part of the $500 million priced in 2020, due in 2025. In the event that free cash flow slumps, Wix might see its excess cash being swept away by the notes, likely requiring it to either refinance with unfavourable debt terms or to convert the notes into equity, resulting in dilution of existing shareholders.

Aside: The company has repurchased notes in recent years but nothing was recorded on its 2024 financial statements.

In essence, we are supportive of Wix's growth and cash flow. However, we are slightly worried about its asset-liability relationship and how that may influence its equity investors' intrinsic value in 2025.

Key Metrics

Rule Of 40

The Rule of 40 basically says that a SaaS company is accretive when its revenue growth + a profit margin (either FCF, operating, or EBITDA) exceeds 40%. Wix's rule of 40 is above 40, suggesting it is creating long-term value. However, the figure is borderline, so we encourage investors to take the metric with a pinch of salt.

Value Creation

Aside from growing an asset base, companies generally create fundamental value by reducing their debt, increasing their EBITDA, and inflating their existing multiples. The following diagram is lagged to Q3's results. However, a time series shows that Wix has consistently reduced its debt-to-equity ratio since early 2022 while sustaining its EBITDA. Moreover, as mentioned earlier, the e-commerce and website hosting domains possess robust growth rates, making interim multiple expansion a possibility.

Return On Capital Requirements & Reinvestment Rate

As with the previous subsection, the following metrics are lagged to Q3. However, as shown, a time series illustrates that Wix's return on invested capital has caught up with its CAPM (or equity investors' expected return), essentially meaning its post-tax operating profit-to-invested capital has caught up with investors' annualised return demands.

Successful companies' expected equity returns generally float above ROIC in earlier years and catch-up at a later stage. The convergence in Wix's scenario is a good sign.

Valuation Multiples

Wix's 2024 full-year revenue settled at a rounded figure of $1.761 billion, its weighted average diluted shares outstanding stood at 59.95 million, and its February 18 stock price closed at $227.78. Collectively, these numbers provide a trailing price-to-sales ratio of around 7.75x, which we consider low for a growth-stage company, I mean, Shopify (SHOP) in comparison has a trailing price-to-sales ratio of 18.78x.

Furthermore, Wix generated $497.42 million in operating cash flow during 2024, and by using the same per-share data as earlier, we derived a price-to-cash flow ratio of 29.44x, which is commendable, given its illustrated operating cash flow growth.

Below is a table compiling the multiples and Wix's relative strength index, a discussion follows.

Metric Value
Price/Sales 7.75x
Price/Cash Flow 29.44x
1-Y RSI 58.04

Source: Author's Work With Wix's Data, Seeking Alpha Used For RSI

The stock's price-to-sales metric was emphasised as Wix remains in a growth stage. Growth-stage companies often focus on market share as opposed to income statement efficiency, making metrics such as price-to-earnings of less use. Moreover, technology companies usually possess subjective amortisation rules and volatile capital structures, meaning down-the-line metrics like price-to-earnings and balance sheet metrics like price-to-book can be misleading.

Furthermore, the stock's price-to-OCF ratio was highlighted as an EBITDA proxy. We highlighted operating cash flow to remove accruals.

Lastly, Wix's RSI was dispensed in the diagram to convey a technical pricing point. As things stand, Wix's RSI remains in the mid-range, suggesting the stock has room to roam.

AI As A Risk Factor

Numerous risks were mentioned throughout the article. However, I wanted to outline AI as a risk factor in a discreet section; here's why.

In our view, the recent progression in AI as a creative tool poses threats to website builders, hosting facilities, and e-commerce intermediaries. AI is affordable and likely to progress into a sphere where fully integrated e-commerce websites can be built at a fraction of Wix's cost. Moreover, AI poses threats to Wix's partners, including web designers, which has fed a lot of growth into the company's revenue base.

While the future of AI is debatable, we highlight it as a leading risk factor for Wix's investors.

Conclusion

The market reacted unfavourably to Wix's fourth-quarter results and outlook. However, we see long-term value creation in store due to factors like Wix's bookings growing substantially, the rule of 40 being breached, debt reduction, and consistent return-on-invested capital increases.

Despite being concerned by risks such as AI and a potential debt-driven cash sweep, we remain bullish about Wix's prospects.

data.title: Jollibee: Watch Internal And External Changes
data.description:
My Buy rating for Jollibee Foods (OTCPK:JBFCY) (OTCPK:JBFCF) (JFC:PS) remains intact after I have assessed developments within and outside the company. Its "coffee and tea businesses" were the subject of the October 5, 2024 write-up. A weaker Philippine peso (or "PHP") and the potential broadening of its shareholder base are positives that support a bullish opinion of the stock.

JBFCF's Tweaks To Its Constitution Make It More Investor-Friendly

JBFCY should be able to attract a greater number of fund managers and individuals to own its shares. This is because the Philippine-listed restaurant operator has initiated favorable changes to its M&AA ("Memorandum and Articles of Association").

Last week, the company disclosed that there won't be any regulatory restrictions on non-Philippine nationals or entities becoming its stockholders going forward. This was made possible by JBFCF's move to do away with its right to hold physical real estate approved at the prior year's AGM ("Annual General Meeting").

The Philippines doesn't want the country's properties to fall into the hands of non-domestic entities. This explains why JBFCY had to cap the proportion of its shares held by foreign investors at 40 percent in the past, in line with the law.

Having secured shareholders' approval to change the company's constitution regarding property ownership, it is no longer bound by this shareholding limit. I think that the stock will benefit from these internal developments.

Jollibee witnessed +$25 million of net foreign fund inflows in H2 2024. This figure is obtained from UBS Group's (UBS) January 6, 2025, report (which isn't available publicly) titled "Consumer: Evidence Lab". According to this investment bank's research, other listed Philippine businesses in the same sector, Universal Robina (OTCPK:UVRBF), Robinsons Retail Holdings (OTCPK:RRETY), and Wilcon Depot suffered from non-domestic investor outflows in the latter half of the previous year.

It is fair to infer from the data presented above that there are international funds who like JBFCF more than other local consumption plays. There's nothing to stop non-Philippine investment firms from acquiring more shares of this listed entity anymore, as I have detailed earlier.

The company's non-domestic shareholder metric was around the 30 percentage (Source: S&P Capital IQ) level on average in the past decade, or below the old 40% cap. Its stock price has a decent chance of rising in the future with the potential for unconstrained foreign investor share purchases.

Also, investors in general will prefer that a company neither engages in real estate speculation nor adopts a capital-heavy operating model. JBFCY's act of changing its M&AA to pivot away from owning property would have eased concerns in these areas. Therefore, it has likely become more shareholder-centric in the eyes of the broader investing community.

Domestic Currency Depreciation Is Good For The Company

It is anticipated that Jollibee will reveal its FY 2024 results and provide guidance for 2025 on March 13 based on S&P Capital IQ's data. My take is that JBFCY will welcome an expected weakening of the PHP, its home currency, that will enhance its prospects for the current year.

The actual US$/PHP rate went from 57.2 on the first day of the current year to 58.2 (Source: S&P Capital IQ) at the time of writing. Economists see the Philippines' currency depreciating further, with this year's forecasts at around the 60 pesos for 1 US dollar mark.

I project that JBFCF's normalized net profit growth will be +24% in FY 2025, which will imply a more rapid pace of expansion vis-à-vis its 2024 bottom line increase of +17% (consensus). My assumption is that foreign exchange tailwinds will help the company to put up an impressive showing in the new year.

The company's businesses outside the Philippines contributed 41% of its most recent Q3 2024 group-level EBITDA. JBFCY's international operations will be expected to report stronger headline figures due to favorable translation effects.

In its local market, an increase in the amount of remittances in PHP terms would offer a boost to consumption. Filipinos are typically proficient in English, so many of them find jobs overseas and then send money back to their families. The strengthening of foreign currencies relative to the peso will mean that consumers at home will have a larger sum of cash inflows, benefiting restaurants run by JBFCF. In fact, close to 97% of monies earned from overseas Filipino workers is utilized for day-to-day needs such as dining out.

Risk Factors To Consider

My major worries for Jollibee relate to the company's desire to engage investors and foreign exchange volatility.

There's no guarantee that international funds will be interested in having a bigger position in the stock, despite the absence of ownership restrictions. It's up to JBFCY to seize the opportunity and ramp up investor relation activities outside its home market.

JBFCF's actual 2025 financial metrics might fall below expectations, assuming that currency movements aren't in its favor. If the PHP appreciates, this implies a less favorable translation impact and lower remittances on a local currency basis.

My Closing Thoughts

JBFCY is deserving of higher valuations and a Buy stock rating.

The company's shares are now trading at 22 times (Source: S&P Capital IQ) forward FY 2025 P/E. Its 3-year historical average earnings multiple is a comparatively richer 30 times. That means that a +36% capital appreciation for the stock is achievable in the event of mean reversion.

Prominent US-listed restaurant names Starbucks (SBUX) and Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) boast even more demanding consensus next twelve months' P/E ratios of 36x and 41x, respectively. As JBFCF widens its pool of international investors, the stock might be able to trade at levels nearer to its peers listed outside of the Philippines. A near-term re-rating catalyst will be profit acceleration in 2025 driven by PHP weakness.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
